data.title: Novavax: Regulatory Uncertainty Creates Risk And Opportunity For Beaten-Down Vaccine Developer
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Novavax (NASDAQ:NVAX) followed the announcement of a transformative collaboration with Sanofi (SNY) to develop COVID/influenza vaccines. The development, which included $500 million upfront and up to $700 million in milestone payments, allowed the struggling vaccine developer to ditch its “going concern” doubts in financial statements. Novavax was also developing its own combination vaccine. Last Fall, Novavax targeted Phase 3 initiation in Q4 2024. The hope is that COVID/influenza combination vaccines will replace seasonal influenza injections. Per Grand View Research, the global influenza vaccine market is valued at nearly $8 billion and is growing at a CAGR of ~7%.

However, Novavax is not alone in this venture. Other vaccine developers, like Pfizer (PFE) and Moderna (MRNA), are developing combination vaccines.

Although the Sanofi collaboration was a positive development, I’ve continued to side with caution on Novavax, rating it a Hold and noting its utility only in a barbell portfolio.

The stock has since fallen 56%.

The article that follows revisits Novavax in light of recent events.

Recent Kennedy Comments Impact Novavax Stock Performance

Novavax stock fell nearly 20% on Thursday after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. cast doubts on Novavax’s COVID vaccination, Nuvaxovid.

Approved for use under the emergency use authorization, the company is in the process of securing full approval. However, the resignation of Dr. Peter Marks, who was a key vaccine regulator, and closer scrutiny of Novavax’s Biologics License Application (BLA) has delayed the FDA’s decision.

Secretary Kennedy, who reportedly spread vaccine misinformation in the past, stated in a recent interview that “single-antigen vaccines have never worked for respiratory illnesses.” Subsequently, under his leadership, the U.S. health authorities are “shifting priorities” towards multiple-antigen vaccines.

Scientific Assessment of Kennedy's Claims

Secretary Kennedy is right about one thing: Novavax’s COVID vaccination is a single-antigen vaccine. To be specific, it is a subunit vaccine that uses a recombinant spike protein from the SARS-CoV-2 virus.

However, he is likely wrong in stating it doesn’t work. Phase 3 data, published in the New England Journal of Medicine, demonstrated “strong evidence of high short-term vaccine efficacy for the prevention of Covid-19 (>90%) and for the prevention of moderate-to-severe disease (100%).” It was also found to be safe.

Moreover, many disagree with Secretary Kennedy’s claim that “single-antigen vaccines have never worked for respiratory illnesses.” Experts point to other COVID vaccines, RSV vaccines, and most influenza vaccines that target a single antigen. However, scientific evidence doesn’t matter as much now as it did before. So, we must assess how this change impacts Novavax’s prospects.

Impact on Sanofi Partnership

Fortunately, Novavax has already transitioned lead commercial responsibility of Nuvaxovid to Sanofi. This will begin with the 2025-2026 vaccination season. Relative to its competitors like Pfizer’s Comirnaty, Nuvaxovid never really caught on in the market, even when COVID was at its worst. Still, Sanofi found it to be of use, particularly in combination with its own influenza vaccines.

Unfortunately for Novavax, a $175 million milestone payment from Sanofi is contingent upon Nuvaxovid’s BLA approval. With this context, it appears that the market is assuming BLA approval is now off the table. Notably, Novavax’s market cap before Thursday’s news was $1.1 billion. So, it dropped by nearly this exact amount.

Current Corporate Strategy

Critically, this shouldn’t have major long-term implications for Novavax’s current corporate strategy. In the past, the company has unsuccessfully tried to independently develop and commercialize its own vaccines. Now, Novavax is prioritizing R&D activities focused on its technology platform and seeking partnerships for both early-stage and late-stage assets. Novavax is still advancing its own COVID/influenza vaccine and stand-alone seasonal influenza vaccine.

Combination Vaccine Development and Competitive Landscape

On that note, Novavax’s COVID/influenza combination vaccine began dosing participants in December 2024 and remains in Phase 3 trial. Initial data is expected by mid-2025.

Moderna is awaiting FDA approval for its mRNA-based vaccine, mRNA-1083. Pfizer and BioNTech (BNTX) experienced a setback in their Phase 3 program after revealing that its mRNA-based combination vaccine failed a key endpoint for the influenza B strain component. So, they could need a reformulation and additional clinical data to support regulatory approval.

Meanwhile, as part of the collaboration, Sanofi is advancing two combination vaccine candidates into Phase 1/2 clinical trials. These candidates combine Novavax’s COVID vaccine with Sanofi’s influenza vaccines. So, in a weird way, Novavax’s internally developed combination vaccine is also “competing” with Sanofi’s.

Novavax is also focused on earlier-stage assets like H5N1 avian pandemic influenza, RSV, shingles, and C. Diff. These are all in preclinical phases.

Earnings

In Q4, total revenues were $88.311 million. This is divided into product sales ($49.774 million) and licensing, royalties, and other ($38.537 million). This was a steep decline from $291.3 million in Q4 2023 due to lower product sales under Advance Purchase Agreements or APAs. Novavax has made steady efforts in cutting operating expenses. Total operating expenses decreased from $474.9 million in Q4 2023 to $219.4 million in Q4 2024. Still, its net loss in Q4 was $81 million.

Looking ahead to 2025, Novavax anticipated combined R&D and SG&A expenses to range from $475 million to $525 million. Adjusted licensing, royalties, and other revenue is expected somewhere between $300 million and $350 million. Notably, this includes the $175 million milestone payment upon the approval of its COVID vaccine BLA.

NVAX Stock Financial Health

As of December 31, cash and equivalents were $530.23 million. Marketable securities totaled $392.888 million. Total current assets were $1.129 billion, while total current liabilities were $1.154 billion. As a result, Novavax’s current ratio is just under 1, implying adequate near-term liquidity. The company does owe $169.684 million in long-term convertible notes.

Because Novavax had a number of one-off payments that have significantly impacted revenues in the recent quarters, I am going to use its expense/revenue estimates as a proxy for cash burn. Its original estimate implied a cash burn of $175 million (at the midpoint) in 2025. If we divide its cash and investments by this figure, it implies a cash runway spanning many years. However, this estimate is based on a number of assumptions (e.g., most of the revenue in 2025 is not recurring). So, there is a lot of nuance in these figures. I will say that if the $175 million milestone payment is thwarted, although this significantly shortens the cash runway, Novavax appears to have the financial flexibility to overcome it.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, Trump administration policies have caused a lot of uncertainty and volatility in financial markets. This is especially true of “vaccine stocks.” Novavax’s BLA delay is now an obvious example of the overreach. We must also not ignore the implications beyond just the Sanofi-related milestone payment. If U.S. health authorities change the rules for vaccine makers mid-game, this could drastically alter the outcomes of all of Novavax’s pursuits. This is an enormous risk. When it comes to rating NVAX, not much has changed fundamentally since my last evaluation. NVAX remains a speculative, high risk/high reward stock that could be of use in a barbell portfolio (maintain Hold). The company finally came to its senses and has altered its path for the good, despite its recent stock performance. Still, the financial (i.e. ongoing net losses, reliance on revenue from partnership milestone payments) and operational (i.e. regulatory, competitive) risks are massive and require careful consideration.

data.title: Danaos: +60% Discount To Book With A Reasonable Yield, Reiterate Buy
data.description:
Although Danaos Corporation (DAC) broke a six-quarter streak of missing EPS estimates, the Greek shipping company came in strong with a 41 bps beat in the final fiscal quarter of 2024. In my last article on DAC back in August 2024, right after Q2 results were announced, I noted that the stock price was far removed from the reality of strong fundamentals. Those fundamentals have held on, but the price seems to have further deteriorated between my last coverage and now.

Even discounting the recent dip-and-rise momentum of the broad market, DAC has mostly been trading sideways but in a very volatile manner. In the meantime, the yield has risen to above 4.7%, which includes the 5-cent increase in quarterly dividends over the past two payout periods.

That deserves a separate discussion now because the stock is literally available for cents on the dollar based on book value, and pays better than risk-free rates. We haven't seen these prices since early 2024.

Re-assessing the Fundamentals of DAC

From a revenue and profitability standpoint, Danaos hasn't done too badly despite the uncertainties that began in the fourth quarter of 2024. Quarterly operating revenues from drybulk show a near doubling, which includes the revenue boost from the additional 5.5 vessels (average) under utilization. Drybulk TCE revenues for the quarter grew almost four-fold on a YoY basis. Meanwhile, overall diluted adjusted EPS came in only marginally lower in Q4 2024 compared to Q4 2023, dropping from $6.99 to $6.93.

I see that overall fleet utilization dropped about 130 bps in Q4, and that's something to keep watching as tariffs continue to dominate the news cycle. It's likely we'll see fleet utilizations drop across all major shippers if and when the tariffs are implemented, but the worry for DAC is their China exposure. Per CEO Dr. John Coustas at the Q4 call:

We'll have to wait until after Chinese New Year to gauge the effect of the front-loading of exports that occurred in anticipation of tariffs and the demand pattern in the new trade environment.

Despite this low visibility into forward revenues, Dr. Coustas is positive on the future of the capesize segment where the company is investing in drybulk vessels. The new vessels delivered in Q2 and later bring its fleet up to 10 drybulk carriers, and despite the additions, utilization came in at 84.2% compared to 81.8% in the year-ago period.

As for their bread and butter segment, containerships, lower per-day TCE rates were mostly offset by 350+ additional operational days and a near-5-count increase in average vessels.

Although net income was down to $505 million in FY 2024 from $576 million in FY 2023, lower SBC, favorable working capital adjustments, and other non-cash items pushed CFO to near $622 million, up from $576 million in the year-ago quarter. That didn't translate to positive free cash flow due to the $659 spent on capex, but with drybulk showing promise even in light of the tariff threats, it's an area of investment that DAC can't afford to pass up.

That said, DAC continues to return to shareholders with the dividend increase, and the bulk of its share buybacks of $53 million was executed in Q4 - about $47.6 million of that, in fact. This is a significant point because it validates how cheap DAC has become in the past two quarters. I think management will keep a heavy foot on the buyback pedal while its stock languishes in the low single digit valuation multiples of 2.5x for its price to forward adjusted EPS and 0.35 for its price to forward book. I do see another board authorization coming up as DAC hits the $168.8 million mark on its current $200 million authorization.

What to Expect for DAC if a Recession Hits

The full-blown recession that many expect if the tariffs are fully implemented is only the beginning of DAC's problems. TCE rates are already showing signs of weakness, and higher rates the drybulk segment just aren't enough to fully offset any further declines. As Dr. Coustas pointed out, China's economy showing very slow recovery is definitely a major concern, and now that we have the makings of an all-out trade war between the U.S. and China, DAC's forward revenues have a big question mark hanging over them.

That being said, I'd still recommend DAC as a good place to park your money. Despite recession fears spooking the market and DAC operating in a segment that's highly prone to disruption by reduced demand for capacity, we need to keep one thing in mind: when the dust settles, shipping companies tend to benefit from new demand channels and better volumes across certain routes. The disruption in the interim, however, is unavoidable.

A further risk is that the threat of tariffs could force several nations targeted by the U.S. to export their wares to other markets in an attempt to offset the decline in U.S. demand. That might not be so simple, however, because the dollar is showing as much resilience as the U.S. economy is.

India seems to be the same. It scrambled to begin negotiations with the Trump administration, try to get the 27% reciprocal tariff down to 10%, per the same Reuters report cited above.

Regardless of how this plays out, the hit to the shipping industry is likely to be significant. Demand is likely to drop dramatically at first, and then gradually increase to fulfil the newly established trade flows. The containerized freight index has dropped significantly in the YTD period, but remains above pre-pandemic levels, so there's some room for play before bottom lines start getting hurt.

Within that larger context, DAC has a lot to offer as a kind of safe-haven investment. Its current assets of $671 million far outweigh the $169 million in short-term liabilities. As mentioned, capex came in very high in FY 2024, but I think it makes sense for DAC to grow its capacity in drybulk, where it can rapidly achieve the kind of scale needed to complement its containership dependency.

The current yield of over 4.7%, combined with the financial strength of the company, its continuing contributions to capesize orderbooks, and a massive $3.4 billion charter backlog will all serve well in the event of a massive trade disruption. The significant potential changes to trade flows and allocations from re-routing of goods will initially take a toll on operating expenses, so margin contraction is something investors will need to be prepared for.

Nevertheless, these headwinds are being met by strong fundamentals on DAC's part.

In the last six months alone, DAC's price to tangible book value multiple has deteriorated by over 23%. I know that DAC has historically traded well below book value, but this is now bordering on the ludicrous. Many investors also balk at Coustas not returning enough to shareholders via dividends, but balance that with their orderbook, huge backlog, aggressive buybacks, and excellent revenue visibility, and what you have is a very compelling risk to reward ratio that favors the upside. Therefore, I continue to rate DAC a Buy. If this valuation imbalance gets worse on the back of severe tariff-led disruptions or a recession, I may re-rate this a Strong Buy.

data.title: Earn Up To 12% Yield While Living The American Dream
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities.

There are over 33 million small businesses in the United States, and while most of them don’t have employees, over 6 million of them employ ~56.4 million workers (in 2021), bringing over $16.2 trillion in revenue. This ecosystem is the engine of the American economy, embodying the essence of the American Dream—the idea that anyone, regardless of background, can achieve success through hard work and perseverance. But this engine needs regular maintenance and upkeep to keep churning out results.

Imagine a locally owned bridal dress business. Orders are placed and deposits are taken months in advance and quoted at a retail price that was based on costs from the designer at the time. But costs are likely to rise with the tariffs on goods and services involved in making the dress. When they are shipped to the bride in the months ahead, they can’t bill her for the extra costs. While larger corporations can absorb interim cost increases while making up for them in future orders, this delta is likely to be of significant impact.

Despite profitability, liquidity is often tight, putting these companies on edge when they need to switch suppliers or expand into adjacent markets. These companies can’t exactly walk into a bank to get a loan, and they are often small with limited revenues or scale to attract the attention of PE (Private Equity) firms. PE firms often focus on larger, more established companies with significant growth potential or restructuring opportunities.

BDCs (Business Development Companies) were instituted by the U.S. Congress in the 1980s with the specific purpose of supporting a large number of small businesses with capital through debt or equity, and management/consulting services to help them through difficult economic conditions. BDCs were originally designed to help the economy recover from a harsh recession. The BDC average debt to equity stood at 118% (well below the 2x limit imposed since the Small Business Credit Availability Act of 2018). Since the limit was raised in 2018, BDCs have operated with ~1.3x leverage, especially in the years following the global pandemic, to take part and promote the economic recovery. We believe that at its current leverage levels, BDCs have attractive opportunities to scale their leverage.

Pick #1: SLRC – Yield 10.9%

SLR Investment Corporation (SLRC) is a unique BDC specializing in equipment financing, ABL (Asset-Based Loans), and sponsor financing. 96.4% of SLRC’s portfolio comprises first-lien senior secured loans, positioning the BDC better to withstand persistent inflationary pressures and high interest rates on the borrowers. SLRC’s loans are tied to assets that were appraised during the time of loan underwriting, and the BDC actively reviews the financial health of its holdings to take necessary action to protect its investment.

Since its IPO in 2010, SLRC has deployed $7.5 billion of investments and has maintained an average loss rate of less than 11 basis points! This has been 15 years of challenging economic conditions, including the aftermath of the GFC, the oil crash of 2015, the global pandemic, and the current elevated interest rate environment. SLRC ended 2024 with a fair market value of ~$2 billion in 890 unique issuers, operating in over 110 industries, and maintaining an average exposure of $3.5 million or 0.1% per issuer.

The company’s full-year NII per share of $1.77 represents a 5% YoY increase, covering its annual dividend ($1.64/share) by 1.1x. This is a remarkable improvement vs. 2022-23, during the period following the SLRC-SUNS merger, when the combined company was not covering its dividend. Q4 marked the sixth straight quarter of NII exceeding dividend payments. SLRC’s year-end NAV per share also increased to $18.20 (vs. $18.09/share), reflecting the overall credit and underwriting quality of the portfolio.

SLRC maintains a 79% allocation to specialty finance investments, and the remainder of the portfolio primarily consists of cash flow loans to borrowers that operate in recession-resistant industries with low CapEx requirements. The company expects that the potential direct impact of tariffs on its portfolio is minimal. The company reports seeing a significant increase in its ABL pipeline. During FY 2024, SLRC had net originations in ABL and Equipment Financing, two solid streams to benefit from higher domestic manufacturing activity. The overall yield of SLRC’s portfolio was 12.1%, reflecting a slight sequential increase from 11.8% in Q3.

As of December 31, the BDC only had one investment on non-accrual status, representing 0.6% of the investment portfolio on a cost basis. The company ended the fiscal year with a net debt-to-equity ratio of 1.03x, on the lower side of its target range of 0.9x to 1.25x. Management expects to move closer to the midpoint of the range in the upcoming quarters, and maintains significant legroom to expand to cater to higher economic activity. The BDC maintains an investment-grade balance sheet, and recently closed two ~50 million private three-year unsecured note offerings at highly competitive fixed interest rates of 6.24% and 6.14%, respectively.

The SLR team owns over 8% of the company's stock, and a significant percentage of the annual incentive compensation of insiders is invested in SLRC stock. This indicates close alignment in the interests of management and shareholders. SLRC is well-positioned to benefit from increased domestic manufacturing activity, given its strengths in senior secured loans with mission-critical assets to back them. As investors, we sit back and collect the well-covered 10.9% yield from this well-managed BDC.

Pick #2: BIZD – Yield 12%

VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) operates with $1.5 billion in net assets, maintaining a market-cap weighted portfolio across 28 public BDCs. This means that the ETF’s composition is highest in the largest BDCs, with Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) representing 22.3% of the invested assets. Source

The ETF’s top five investments are in BDCs with rather low debt-to-equity ratios, with ARCC and Main Street Capital (MAIN) at 102% and 76%, respectively. This means that the blue-chip BDCs have adequate legroom to expand their portfolios to lend and seize opportunities should the economy slip into a recession or experience challenges.

BIZD has paid steady quarterly dividends since its inception while maintaining a steady price level over time. The ETF’s current dividend annualizes to a 12% yield, higher than historic payout levels.

A $10,000 investment in BIZD in 2016 would have generated a 9.9% average annual yield over the years. Source

A weakening economy presents opportunities for BDCs to support small and middle-market businesses, while collecting attractive interest income in the process. With BIZD, you buy the haystack and profit from the powerhouses fueling the growth of small businesses, embodying the spirit of the American Dream.

Note: When it comes to FOFs (Fund of Funds), the expense ratio can be a bit misleading due to regulatory requirements. The net expense is broken down into direct and indirect expenses. Direct expenses are those expenses and fees that accrue daily and are paid monthly. These expenses are borne directly by the fund and reduce its net assets, thus detracting from total return. Management fees, salaries, and bonuses are part of direct expenses.

Indirect expenses: SEC rules require a FOF to report a total expense ratio in its prospectus fee table that accounts for the expense ratios of the underlying funds (in this case the underlying BDCs) in which it invests as an expense item called acquired fund fees and expenses. AFFEs are not accrued daily, nor are they paid directly from the Fund’s net assets. Despite BIZD’s net expense ratio showing 13.33%, investors should note that only 0.42% comprises direct expenses (and which are not subtracted from your dividend payment). BIZD Expenses Explained

Conclusion

Small businesses are the lifeblood of the U.S. economy, fueling innovation, job creation, and economic growth. BDCs are a vital source of capital for this institution, making them the lifelines. By investing in SLRC, investors gain exposure to a well-managed, high-yield BDC with a strong track record of risk management and disciplined underwriting. BIZD, on the other hand, offers a diversified approach, allowing investors to collect regular income from the broader strength of the BDC sector.

Trillions of dollars are transacted every day. At High Dividend Opportunities, we tap into this mighty ocean of capital flows and direct a stream of income into our accounts. We do this through a diversified portfolio of over 42 holdings, with components across industry sectors. This strategy allows us to sit back, enjoy financial independence, and retire on our terms. This is the power of income investing—steady, reliable, and built for long-term success.

data.title: Tencent: Follow AI Money To China Amid Tariff Panic - Buy Below $54
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) is an all-in-one tech and internet kind of Chinese company, focusing on video games in specific. I see a buying opportunity in this Chinese player after it shed 20% on the month chart, mainly because it's pumping funds into AI that I'm expecting to pay off big time.

Tencent stock has been getting hammered, especially with the latest tariff announcements on Liberation Day, like you can see below. I'm looking at this drop as a buying opportunity because I think the stock is cheap at current levels, now at $54 per share, for a company on the verge of incorporating AI into every aspect of its business and is definitely putting down the money for it with a hefty increase in capex for this year. The revenue split is over five segments: Marketing, domestic games, international games, social networks, and fintech. With a reported revenue of 660 billion CN¥, approximately $90 billion, for 2024, up 8% from the year prior, I see more upside when AI is more seriously added to their mix.

What's Tencent doing about AI?

Tencent has been building its AI capabilities since 2023, what they've called the "Huyeuan Foundation Model." Huyeuan is essentially a large language model, similar to ChatGPT, and, yes, Tencent is not the only one in the AI game, but it holds the advantage when it comes to imaging and video capabilities. The HY model can generate 3D images and videos in seconds, in what is an industry first. It outperforms both Deepseek (DEEPSEEK) and ChatGPT across most AI benchmarks.

Now, the second advantage for Tencent is the fact that they have the supply and demand at a time when we're seeing global panic about AI demand trends. The company plugs its AI model into its own businesses, particularly advertising, with new personalized 3D AI-generated ads on the way.

Advertising/Marketing - Now, I'm most excited about AI in advertising because that's Tencent's fastest-growing market, as shown below. Advertising revenue is growing, up to 20% of their total revenue in 4Q24; that's up from 14% in 4Q22 and 19% in 4Q23. Revenue from marketing has seen an average of 1-2% quarterly growth over the past 3 years. It's actually the only business line that's steadily growing as a total percent of revenue, and I think it'll be over 20% of total revenue by the end of the year because of the HY model. The HY model can trace users' behavior across gaming platforms and their connected social media. With this data collected, the model then sets about creating a personalized AI-generated commercial for your product targeted at each user. This is a service that Tencent sells and uses itself across other businesses lines.

Gaming - The thing about Tencent that most people don't understand is the fact that this is a company that throws ads into all aspects of their business: gaming platforms and social networks, and even in Fintech, they offer the chance to display ads, and gaming is a huge opportunity for this. Just last year, Tencent was ranked top in games publishers by worldwide consumer spend, shown below. They also became "the highest—grossing publisher." Now, they're expanding their international footprint in the face of increased competition with the likes of Microsoft (MSFT); Tencent owns League of Legends, which boasts 180 million active players, and offering a new advertising product on this platform may see significant demand.

Tencent plans to use the model to enhance their 3D graphics in games, while also adding chatbots into games for users to interact in real-time with gaming characters. This offers a huge incentive for users to play more Tencent games in China. Just like marketing their domestic game revenue segment, it contributes to 20% of the total revenue, or about $4.6 billion, this quarter. The latest numbers from 2024 indicate there are approximately 668 million active gamers in China, just over half of which play some kind of Tencent game. The gaming market over there is currently valued at over $40 billion, with the average gamer spending about $60 on games. Investors should keep their eye on the marketing revenue number as I expect it to see an increase in the next quarterly earnings on May 14th.

Aside from AI-specific focus, the company's also making moves to grow internationally. In recent news, rumors have been going around that Tencent wants to expand its international games business. At the end of March, Ubisoft announced that they were creating a new gaming subsidiary with Tencent. Tencent should acquire a 25% stake in this new subsidiary. The good news is, that this new subsidiary includes Ubisofts most popular games, like Far Cry, Assassin's Creed, and Rainbow Six. What does this mean exactly, well, Tencent will essentially pay $1.25 billion for royalties, and this new entity will hold licenses for the intellectual properties of the game mentioned. This is good timing on Tencent's behalf, with the likes of Assassin's Creed Shadows just being released a couple of weeks ago. The new Assassin's Creed is reported to be a big hit, with Ubisoft claiming the game reached 3 million players last week.

Fintech and business services - This makes up the bulk of their revenues. Revenue in fintech continued its slow year-over-year growth, with a 4% increase in 2024. Two of their office tech tools, WeCom and Tencent Meetings, saw strong growth in terms of user base. WeCom, an office chat tool, doubled its year-over-year revenue, while Tencent Meetings increased its revenue by 40% in 2024. The domestic sentiment in China is moving more inwards, and I think this could be a boost for the use of Tencent's alternative platform to Google or Microsoft's office tools.

Follow the Money, Follow the capex

Investors always panic when they see high capex, but that's because of what I call tunnel vision. Tencent got some backlash announcing another big capex number for 2025 on their 4Q24 report; however, I think increased profits signal that Tencent is putting money where it's expecting to see a return. Last quarter's earnings for Tencent were released in mid-March, causing the stock to drop 8% drop on that panic. 4Q24 revenue came in at 172 billion CN¥, beating Wall Street’s consensus by 2%. Company revenue also grew 11% year-over-year. What overshadowed these numbers was Tencent’s increased capital expenditure on AI for 2025, expected to be between 13% and 15%. This capex spending on AI is following the same trend as last year; about 10% of the 2024 revenue was invested in AI. To put that in context, only 3.4% and 2.6% of the yearly revenue went into new investments in 2023 and 2022, respectively. So, we're seeing management focus more on channeling funds to prop its AI position, definitely prioritizing that spend over others, with the company announcing a 30% reduction in share buybacks for 2025. It looks like the money saved from the drop in buybacks will be pumped into AI for longer-term growth. It takes money to make money, and while that's the saying, that goes for the average joe. It also applies to the big companies like Tencent, without spend you can't see return, and so I'm taking the higher capex for the year as a reason to run to the stock, not away from it.

Not mention that the reason Tencent is upping its AI related spend is because they need the capacity, aka they're seeing high demand. While the AI and cloud computing segment has approximately doubled year-over-year for Tencent, they claim that an increasing usage of GPUs for internal use cases, which stops them from selling GPUs to external clients, is holding them back. In the latest earnings call transcript, they mention the demand is there for all their computing services, but due to a GPU shortage, some orders had to be turned away. They have since claimed this issue will be fixed with a higher order of GPUs in 2025, a statement that was made for the first time in March’s earnings. This gives me confidence that the GPU shortage should be resolved, and we should expect this to show up in earnings over the next one to two quarters.

Bringing AI to the table for everyone

In terms of other AI products, it seems Tencent dipped its toes into just about everything, poised for a nice boost off the back of the next AI boom. What they can offer to businesses is called a Hyper Computing Cluster (HCC). This allows other businesses to run AI models with fast processing and scalability. Alongside this is the Hyper Application Inventor (HAI), essentially a tool for developers to deploy AI tools quickly and easily. To give a quick example, any company might be looking into incorporating AI into their website or business, Tencent can offer the AI computing power to do this through the HCC and HAI.

Additionally, anyone looking for a platform to develop their own AI models can take advantage of HAI without actually being that well-versed in AI due to the interactive visual elements of the new platform. I'm expecting such a product will be a big hit with small to mid-level businesses wanting to incorporate some AI into their product offering. These two services would be in high demand because of the current AI boom.

Valuation

Tencent's forward price to sales is 5.12 versus the sector median at 1.03, with forward EV sales at 5.26 at 1.81. The stock isn't cheap from a valuation perspective, but I think this can be considered as a growth pick with the heavy AI capex. The company is down 44% from its all-time high. The market cap is roughly $500 billion. The stock price is at $54, and last quarter's earnings release hurt the stock due to that increased capex, the stock was down 8% until Liberation Day, which knocked it a further 14%.

What's Next?

Investors should recontextualize Tencent's latest capex investment in AI because if you're looking one year out, it's a positive in my book. The new tech they are developing will come around and go straight back into their current business segment with new targeted AI ads and gaming chatbots. AI-powered interactive chatbots will boost the number of active users. A growing user base will fuel demand for personalized AI ads on their platforms. There was a recent drop in stock price after Liberation Day; investors should see this as an opportunity to get Tencent stock at a discount. Whether to jump into the stock now or wait until China hits back with further tariffs is up to the investor, which they did this morning with 84% tariffs on the U.S., I think at $54 per share, it's a good time to start a position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mixed Results, Clear Signals: Navigating Resource Equities In A Shifting Macro Landscape
data.description:
Commodity markets face uncertainty from tariffs, global growth risks and geopolitics, but may show resilience. Tight supply and global stimulus support a constructive long-term outlook.

Despite outperforming broad U.S. equities, resource equities delivered mixed results in the first quarter of 2025. Base metals, precious metals and natural gas producers led the way, supported by strong underlying commodity prices—most notably copper, gold and natural gas. In contrast, most oil producers struggled as crude prices retreated below $70/bbl.

The dominant macro themes during the quarter included the potential downstream impact of the Trump Administration’s tariffs on commodity markets and resource equities, as well as an increasingly uncertain global growth outlook.

Sector Performance Recap

  • Gold – Gold and gold mining stocks were the top performers during the quarter. Gold prices advanced above $3,100 per ounce, driven by sustained central bank buying, a resurgence in gold ETF demand and heightened geopolitical risk. Gold miners benefited from record margin expansion, supported by elevated gold prices and relatively stable operating costs.
  • Base Metals – Copper led the strength in base metals, rallying approximately 25% during the quarter. Most base metal miners saw share price gains in response. Trade policy dominated headlines, as the Trump Administration implemented 25% tariffs on imported aluminum and steel, while also floating the possibility of similar duties on copper. Ongoing skepticism around China’s ability to reinvigorate its economy remained a headwind for the sector overall.
  • Oil & Gas – Oil prices faced significant pressure during the quarter, falling below $70/bbl after OPEC+ signaled its intentions to ease production caps that had been in place for over two years. Further weighing on the sector was a deteriorating global growth outlook, with the International Energy Agency downgrading its oil demand forecasts. Natural gas producers fared better, buoyed by modest price gains and growing optimism around gas’ role in meeting incremental electricity demand.
  • Agriculture – Agricultural commodities and equities posted modest gains during the quarter. Supportive fertilizer prices and resilient crop prices underpinned performance. Agricultural machinery stocks also fared well, defying headwinds from "higher for longer" interest rates and the potential drag from upcoming trade tariffs.

Looking Ahead: Navigating an Evolving Landscape

Our outlook, written before the April 2 announcement of new tariffs by the Trump Administration, anticipates continued challenges and uncertainty in commodity markets driven by trade policies, geopolitical tensions and broader macroeconomic trends. While tariffs remain a significant concern, their broader impact on global economic growth is the more pressing issue for commodities. As we assess the landscape, we see resilience in key commodities, despite the ongoing risks.

The uncertainty introduced by tariffs influences both global economic activity and commodity demand. If tariffs slow economic growth, they could weigh on demand for key raw materials. However, commodities have endured similar cycles before, and market participants are prepared to adjust as needed.

Steel and aluminum remain focal points in the tariff discussion. U.S. steel producers have already responded by increasing prices, benefiting from the anticipated trade barriers. However, outside the U.S.—particularly in Canada, Mexico and Europe—producers could face headwinds. Energy markets have also absorbed some of the uncertainty. A 10% tariff on Canadian and Mexican energy imports is not expected to be a major disruptor, as price adjustments have already taken place. However, crude oil has struggled relative to other commodities, reflecting broader geopolitical uncertainties and shifts in global supply and demand. Meanwhile, natural gas has emerged as a more attractive opportunity, particularly within the U.S.

Beyond tariffs, a series of external risks continue to challenge commodities. Geopolitical tensions—ranging from conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East to instability in Venezuela and OPEC+ production decisions—create a difficult operating environment. These factors have combined to form one of the most complex commodity landscapes in recent memory. Despite these risks, the resilience of commodity markets has been notable. China and Europe continue to roll out stimulus measures, helping to support demand even in the face of economic slowdowns. Whether global growth weakens significantly or stabilizes remains to be seen, but for now, many commodities are holding firm.

Despite the macroeconomic pressures, several commodities remain strong. Gold has reached record highs, underscoring its role as a safe-haven asset amid market uncertainty. Copper prices are also robust, reflecting tight supply conditions and long-term demand tied to electrification trends. Even agricultural commodities, which face potential challenges from Chinese retaliatory tariffs, have remained reasonably stable. In contrast, crude oil continues to lag behind, likely due to geopolitical uncertainty and shifting supply dynamics. This divergence has led to strategic shifts in positioning, with some investors reducing oil exposure in favor of U.S. natural gas, which presents a more favorable outlook. Additionally, selective steel exposure has been added, recognizing both tactical opportunities and long-term structural trends.

While commodity markets face considerable uncertainty, their resilience in the face of multiple headwinds is noteworthy. The interplay between tariffs, economic growth, and geopolitical risks will continue to shape the market, requiring investors to remain flexible. With supply conditions tight across several key commodities and demand supported by global stimulus efforts, the longer-term outlook remains constructive despite near-term challenges.

Important Disclosure

Please note that VanEck may offer investments products that invest in the asset class(es) or industries included in this blog.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third-party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

Hard assets investments are subject to risks associated with real estate, precious metals, natural resources and commodities and events related to these industries, foreign investments, illiquidity, credit, interest rate fluctuations, inflation, leverage, and non-diversification.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

© Van Eck Associates Corporation666 Third Avenue | New York, NY 10017

data.title: Warning For SCHD Investors: Tariffs Are A Major Risk
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola

The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) is one of the most popular dividend growth ETFs in the world. Here, you can see the rapid growth in its assets under management in recent years:

This growing popularity is the result of the ETF's amazing track record, having managed to pay high and growing dividends year after year, all while keeping up with the total returns offered by the Tech-heavy S&P500 (SPY):

We generally like the ETF and have previously even given it a Buy rating in our previous coverage.

But now, there are growing fears that SCHD could suffer as a result of the major tariffs recently imposed by President Trump.

Unlike the S&P500, which is heavily invested in tech stocks, the SCHD ETF is mostly invested in more traditional higher-yielding companies that sell physical goods.

This is a major disadvantage in the case of a trade war, given that physical goods are much easier to tariff than intangible services.

In what follows, I will discuss Trump's latest tariff announcements and why it is bad news for most stocks, but especially for SCHD.

Tariffs Are Bad News for Global Companies

Just last week, President Trump announced sweeping tariffs on the entire world, with America's biggest trading partners being hit especially hard.

Then, a few days ago, he adjusted his plan by lowering the tariffs to 10% on most countries for the next 90 days while increasing the tariffs on China to a whopping 125%!

According to Bloomberg, the overall impact on the US economy isn't expected to be much different from the earlier plan, given that the average US tariff rate dropped from 27% to 24%:

This will hurt big international US companies like those held in the SCHD ETF in many ways:

  • 1) It will increase the cost of imports, potentially hurting their profit margins.
  • 2) It will make them less competitive on the global stage. As an example, with China imposing 84% tariffs on US goods, it is likely that Chinese consumers will buy more Japanese and Korean goods rather than American.
  • 3) The increased uncertainty could lower business confidence and cause companies to delay major investments and hiring, hurting the broader economy.
  • 4) It could also have indirect effects with the world deciding to boycott US goods and US tourism. The advance flight bookings from Canada to the US are down 70%, as an example.
  • 5) It also weakens the US Dollar, increasing the cost of imports even further, and also increases the cost of capital for US companies.

So, overall, it is hard to argue how this would benefit international US companies.

But how do SCHD's holdings fare against the average US company?

Unfortunately, I fear that SCHD could be especially badly impacted, and this is why it is selling off right now.

Tariffs Are Very Bad News for SCHD

The tariffs were announced a week ago, and here is how SCHD has performed since then:

It has done even worse than the S&P500 (SPY), and there's a good reason for it:

SCHD has more exposure to traditional, good-based businesses, and I expect those to be more heavily impacted by the tariffs.

Let's just look at some of its top 10 holdings:

  1. Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) - 4.39%
  2. The Coca-Cola Company (KO) - 4.37%​
  3. ConocoPhillips (COP) - 4.25%
  4. PepsiCo Inc. (PEP) - 4.17%​
  5. Chevron Corporation (CVX) - 4.16%
  6. Altria Group Inc. (MO) - 4.11%​
  7. Amgen Inc. (AMGN) - 4.07%​
  8. Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) - 4.04%
  9. The Home Depot, Inc. (HD) - 4.01%​
  10. Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) - 3.95%

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) are very recognizable brands that most foreign consumers associate with the USA. It makes it very easy to boycott, and the switching costs are low. If the price of Coca-Cola goes up significantly in China, a lot of consumers will simply switch to a lower-cost alternative that tastes similar.

The energy companies Chevron (CVX) and ConocoPhillips (COP) will suffer from the now much lower commodity prices. Energy prices have crashed to much lower level, and this will affect their profitability.

The big pharmaceutical companies like Amgen (AMGN) and Bristol (BMY) also generate a substantial portion of their revenue from foreign countries, and the current administration thinks that it is especially important to bring back their production to the US to not rely on foreign countries for medicine. That's a fair point, but it will come at a cost, likely reducing profits and making them less competitive on the global stage.

Home Depot (HD) is importing a ton of stuff from abroad, so its cost will go up, and if the economy dips into a recession, people will not move as much, let alone spend money on home renovations.

Finally, defense companies like Lockheed Martin (LMT) are crashing because the US is losing the trust of its allies, and as a result, they are also less likely to buy American weapons. As an example, President Trump recently suggested that the future American fighter jets sold abroad should be toned down in case today's allies are not allies in the future anymore. Some countries like Portugal have already responded to this by cancelling their orders, and the European Union is now planning to drastically increase its own weapons production.

Bottom Line

The common narrative is that SCHD is "more defensive and less volatile", but in the context of a trade war, it could be the opposite since it owns a lot of companies that produce physical goods.

  • They are easy to tariff
  • Require tangible supply chains
  • A lot of raw materials
  • and/or have relatively low switching costs
  • and/or are easy to boycott
  • and/or suffer greatly in a recession

Therefore, I would argue that in a direct trade war, SCHD will have even more risk than something like the S&P500, which is more heavily invested in tech companies.

Those will suffer as well, but many of its top holdings sell software, services, cloud, and intellectual property, which are harder to tariff.

As an example, Meta's (META) Instagram, Facebook, WhatsApp, and other social media will still be used just as much as before by Europeans.

Moreover, to the extent that they produce physical goods, the switching costs are also higher. Think of Apple's iPhones as an example. Even if its cost goes up 20% or even 30%, I would still buy one because I am now stuck with its operating system, and the extra cost is manageable since I don't change my phone frequently anyway.

Same for Microsoft's (MSFT) Office 365. Even if its cost went way up, people and businesses would still buy it. You are locked into the ecosystem, and the switching costs are very high.

But the switching costs are not that high for the goods sold by a lot of SCHD's top holdings. If their price increases too much, consumers will simply switch to a cheaper alternative, and it won't have any real impact on their lives.

The bottom line is that tariffs hurt more physical goods that are easy to replace, and therefore, I fear that SCHD is more vulnerable to this trade war than many appear to understand.

If you think that this will be over soon, now could be a good time to buy the dip. But if you think that these are still the early days of a nasty trade war, I would consider getting out of it while it is only down about 10% from its recent highs.

At High Yield Investor, we prefer to selectively invest in defensive high-yielding stocks like MLPs (AMLP) and REITs (VNQ) that own essential infrastructure that's not affected by the tariffs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Consumer Is Tapping Out
data.description:
The recent implementation of tariffs has the media buzzing about increased recession odds as the consumer faces potentially higher costs. While recent economic reports, like the latest employment report, still show robust growth, those data points run with a lag that hasn’t yet caught up with reality.

As we have discussed, the American consumer is the backbone of the U.S. economy and comprises nearly 70% of the GDP calculation. While GDP surged following the economic shutdown due to the massive flood of stimulus that fueled a savings surge, consumption as a percent of the economy has remained flat since the turn of the century. The reason is that despite the surge in savings, the consumer was also faced with rising inflation, which left them struggling to make ends meet.

This dilemma is better illustrated by the chart below. The blue line is the personal savings rate, and the red line shows the debt needed annually to bridge the gap between the inflation-adjusted cost of living and savings and incomes. As shown, at the turn of the century, the consumer was no longer able to fund their living standard through just income and savings. The fact that consumers were forced to take on increasing debt levels to maintain their living standards explains why consumption as a percent of GDP has remained stagnant over the same period.

At the heart of the problem is the collapse of household balance sheets in the lower-income and middle-income brackets. These groups have depleted the excess savings accumulated during the pandemic and are turning to high-interest borrowing to bridge the gap. The Philadelphia Federal Reserve reported that the share of active credit card accounts making only minimum payments surged to 10.75% in Q3 2024—a record high. This statistic isn’t just a warning about credit health; it points to widespread cash flow stress.

In addition, more consumers are falling behind on their monthly card payments. The balance-based 30+ days past due rate increased 33 basis points year-over-year to 3.52% in the third quarter of 2024. This represents more than double the delinquency rate of 1.57 percent at the pandemic low in the second quarter of 2021.

More alarming is the growing use of Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) services. Notably, those services are not being used for large discretionary purchases but for food.

Recent surveys show that more consumers are increasingly relying on installment payment platforms like Klarna and Affirm to afford meals. Initially, the design of the BNPL model was for luxury or semi-durable goods. However, its expansion into groceries signals deep-rooted affordability issues. Debt is no longer just a tool for convenience; it’s a necessity for millions’ survival.

The problem with Trump’s trade war now is that it comes when consumers are already showing clear signs of distress. According to recent data, both from the Federal Reserve and corporate earnings reports, the consumer’s financial cushion that kept consumer spending alive in 2021 and 2022 is gone. What remains is a fragile consumer base increasingly reliant on credit and debt to afford necessities. While inflation has slowed, its damage is lingering. Now there is growing evidence suggesting that a recession and deflation are more immediate risks.

Consumer Confidence Declining

Consumer stress isn’t limited to anecdotal indicators—it’s now showing up in corporate earnings and executive commentary. During the company’s earnings call, Doug McMillon, CEO of Walmart, stated that many customers are under “budget pressure.” They are also exhibiting “stressed behaviors,” including spending reductions across general merchandise. Specifically, he warned that “For many customers, the money runs out before the month does.”

Similarly, Dollar General CEO Todd Vasos painted an equally concerning picture. He described his customers as “struggling more than ever before.” Todd added that some are now forgoing non-discretionary items, like medication or hygiene products, to afford groceries and fuel. He said, “These customers are making trade-offs we haven’t seen in years.” Concurring with that warning was Jane Fraser, CEO of Citigroup. She observed that consumers are “becoming more cautious” and focusing spending on smaller, lower-cost purchases. While this signals a growing defensive posture, often associated with recessionary conditions, they are also deflationary. When consumer behavior shifts en masse from aspirational to survival-based, the ripple effects are inevitable.

When we combine all the various measures of confidence into a single index, the correlation to GDP is unsurprising.

Furthermore, that decline in confidence leads to changes in the rate of inflation. This should be unsurprising since prices reflect supply and demand. As demand declines, prices fall to levels where demand for those products, goods, or services exists.

The data supports this narrative. Real personal consumption expenditures, the most significant component of GDP, are weakening. Once optimistic, the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model has revised estimates lower. Such was due to the decline in spending on goods and services. High interest rates, implemented by the Federal Reserve to curb inflation, now exert a secondary effect. Those rates are strangling credit access and making existing debt more expensive.

Housing data also reflects economic strain. Residential building permits and starts have declined markedly over the past six months, and homebuilder confidence has also deteriorated. First-time homebuyers—often a leading indicator of broader consumer strength—have retreated sharply due to affordability concerns.

When combined with increased pressures from higher taxes (read tariffs), the data is sending a warning.

The Risk Of Recession (and Deflation) Have Increased Markedly

The current data point toward a recessionary risk. Deflation is highly correlated to economic growth rates, wages, and rates. Unsurprisingly, recessions reduce inflation as demand for goods and services collapses. While inflation may be “sticky,” the recent decline in bond yields and wages suggests consumer demand will decline this year.

When tariffs, an additional tax on consumers, increase the cost burden, the reaction historically is not expansionary. As consumers contract spending, employers reduce business investment (demand) and cut employment (supply of wages). As shown, while volatile, plans to expend capital for investment purposes correlate with real private investment (which feeds into GDP.) While this data does not currently reflect the tariff impact, it was already suggesting much weaker growth. We suspect the outlook for CapEx has declined markedly in recent weeks.

We are seeing “demand destruction” caused by rising input costs due to tariffs against an already weak consumer backdrop. That combination of inputs will likely lead to higher unemployment, slower growth, and deflationary pressures in the economy unless there is a supply shock due to some unforeseen event like another “oil embargo.” Outside of such an event, in an environment where consumer demand is falling due to the inability to afford what’s available, suppliers will have to cut prices to find buyers.

Furthermore, credit conditions also reinforce the recession risk. Banks have tightened lending standards across consumer and commercial lines as credit card delinquencies have ticked up sharply, particularly among borrowers aged 18–39. The Federal Reserve’s Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey shows a continued reduction in credit availability—making it even harder for stretched consumers to borrow their way through.

This reflects a critical turning point: the U.S. consumer is no longer a driver of economic growth but a potential drag on it. When nearly 70% of GDP depends on consumption, a weakening consumer poses systemic risks. A policy pivot may be necessary, and the calls for further Fed rate cuts this year are rising, with markets expecting four rate cuts this year. However, for now, with inflation still above target and the labor market gradually cooling, policymakers lack the room to cut rates aggressively without potentially reigniting price pressures. However, as the impact of tariffs causes a marked reduction in demand, those fears will likely give way to concerns about economic disruption.

In short, the American consumer is tapped out. The savings buffer is gone, wage growth is declining, and credit costs are rising. Corporate America is already adjusting to this new reality, with companies issuing cautious guidance for 2025. Even the tech sector—previously resilient—is showing signs of demand compression in consumer-facing verticals.

Unless wage growth accelerates or interest rates decline meaningfully, the pressure on households will continue to mount. That means recession and, ultimately, deflation—the more immediate threat to the U.S. economy. While deflation may seem the “out of consensus” view – if demand destruction continues unchecked, the more pressing concern is a downturn in demand. Declining real incomes and credit exhaustion are already warning of that risk.

Investors and policymakers would do well to focus less on inflation in isolation and more on the consumer’s deteriorating balance sheet. That’s where the next economic shock is currently hiding.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Taking Apart The Tariff Impact With ETFs
data.description:
A large consensus has been to stay the course with long-term portfolio goals despite recent tariff volatility. While this is generally true, short-term fluctuations still matter. We have had plenty of those recently - by the time you read this note, the market could be either up or down. Regardless, it’s still important to talk about these fluctuations - even if they do disappear in the long term. These are some thoughts on why investors need to pay attention to tariffs, implications for the market and ETFs, and what this could mean for the future.

Fundamentals vs. Sentiment: Both are important

There is a never-ending battle between fundamentals and sentiment. Many investment professionals stand by fundamentals (i.e., examining information like a company’s earnings statements and macroeconomic data to forecast long-term growth). While sentiment is not always based on real facts, its effect on portfolios is always real. It is easy to brush off sentiment because of this and urge investors to look long term. But short-term fluctuations are difficult emotionally (red is an infuriating color to see). Part of investment research is providing investment recommendations. But it’s also providing education on why the market makes moves and what precedent is set for the future.

Uncertainty persists - Expect more wild swings

Right now, tariffs are synonymous with uncertainty. There are many layers to the tariffs - baseline, reciprocal, retaliatory, certain items have separate tariffs, certain items are exempt, etc. It is also uncertain how much can be negotiated and how much is set in stone. We recently saw the extreme side of it when China was hit with a 125% tariff (and we were hit with an 84% tariff in return). With all of these moving parts, it is almost impossible to parse the total economic impact. Companies, e.g., Delta Airlines (DAL), are starting to cut or withdraw their full-year outlooks. In the investment world, the unknown can have an even bigger impact than the known. Until we have more certainty on the impact, the market will likely see these wide swings and continue to be news-driven.

Not all equities are built the same

Broadly, U.S. equities have fallen even further after a tough 1Q. While it has been red across the board, certain sectors have been more affected than others. Growthier sectors like technology and consumer discretionary tend to pull back whenever there is economic weakness. That is the case here, but technology and consumer discretionary stocks also have the added factor of heavily leaning on manufacturing - particularly in Asia. Year to date, the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) was down 23% YTD. The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) was down 21%.

On the discretionary side, Nike (NKE), for example, has over 50% of its products produced in China and Vietnam. On the technology side, it’s interesting to note that semiconductors are exempt from reciprocal tariffs. But end-products that use semiconductors, like Apple (AAPL) iPhones, are affected by tariffs. And that results in a secondary effect. The VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) is down 26% YTD despite being a recent investor favorite in 2024.

On the defensive side, it is more about resilience rather than opportunities - and consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities have been somewhat resilient. The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) is down only 3% YTD. The dynamic between staples and discretionary is always interesting during economic swings. That is because consumer staples businesses typically have more pricing power and can pass on those higher costs. Consumers typically cut back on discretionary spending in favor of essentials.

What does this mean? Even if you don’t have specific allocations to technology or discretionary names, be wary of overexposure to broad domestic equity ETFs, which typically have a heavy concentration to these sectors. For example, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) has a 29% weight to technology. And the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) has a 33% weight to technology.

1Q put more eyes on international - now that’s getting more complex

While the U.S. market saw a pullback in 1Q, international equity was thriving. Now international markets are also seeing a pullback. For reference, most of the deeply affected international ETFs are those with the highest tariffs - China and Vietnam. The iShares China Large-Cap ETF (FXI) is down 17% since April 2. And the VanEck Vietnam ETF (VNM) is down 20% during the same period. On a year-to-date basis, however, the pullback is less severe relative to the U.S. Year to date, FXI is down 3%, VNM is down 12%, and SPY is down 15%.

Some resilient spots exist internationally. Mexico - along with Canada - escaped the broad-based tariffs due to some existing trade agreements in North America. The iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW) is currently up 3% YTD. Additionally, the iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (EWZS) is up 7% YTD. Brazil escaped with “only” a 10% tariff. That gives it an edge, assuming it can take advantage of its position this year.

International exposure will be a test for retail investors. With many retail investors recently adding to their international exposures in 1Q to counter the U.S. equity pullback, they may be tempted to exit. But note that YTD, most international equity has still held up better than U.S. equity, and diversification shouldn’t be timed for convenience.

Diversifiers work

Also regarding diversification, Todd Rosenbluth, VettaFi’s head of research, recently wrote a note on portfolio diversifiers. As with international equities, hindsight is always 20/20. Investors often think of buying diversifiers after the fact, instead of holding steady in a portfolio. It is recommended to hold a diversified portfolio to weather through all cycles of the economy - not just after the fact. While gold - the most well-known safe haven asset - also fell during the initial sell-off, it has held up more resiliently relative to equities. The SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) is up 14% year-to-date.

Opporunities to buy the dip (or 3x that)

It is rational to be afraid of volatility, but volatility can also create more opportunities. For certain areas that have strong long-term fundamentals - like semiconductors - the pullback could be an opportunity to buy the dip. Notably, Cathie Wood is finally buying Nvidia (NVDA) again for her flagship ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK).

Additionally, some traders and investors are embracing the volatility and piling into leveraged products. The ProShares UltraPro QQQ (TQQQ) and the Direxion Daily Semiconductors Bull 3x Shares (SOXL) - both 3x leveraged ETFs - saw a large amount of inflows earlier this week. For certain investors, making a leveraged bet in a market with wide swings is more attractive than simply buying the dip.

ETFs provide many of these tools to investors

Whether investors are hedging their risks or buying the dip, ETFs continue to be an attractive investment choice across various strategies. U.S. ETFs saw $24.7 billion in net inflows over the past week with a mix of equities, fixed income, and even 3x leveraged plays.

Final Thoughts: Are tariffs good or bad?

I don’t believe tariffs are good or bad. They are tools to control imports, manufacturing and supply chain footprints and theoretically can boost long-term growth. But this doesn’t come easy. The long-term trend has been moving from offshoring to nearshoring to reshoring. Offshoring was very popular for many years. It provided cheap labor and access to different parts of the global markets (i.e., access to more growth areas), while the U.S. focused on high-value service sector jobs.

This started trending to nearshoring, or moving manufacturing to nearby areas like Mexico, where labor is still cheap but supply chains are more efficient. From there, the pandemic was a catalyst to reshoring - returning manufacturing directly to the U.S. The argument here was that we needed to bring back manufacturing jobs to the U.S. and simplify supply chains to avoid disruption. While U.S. labor costs more than foreign labor, other potential benefits may counteract the costs; for example, cheaper transportation costs, reduced U.S. trade deficit, and a stronger U.S. dollar.

But implementation is often difficult. I spent a large part of my career analyzing supply chain companies, and supply chains are complex and don’t change overnight. Companies must deal with higher shipping costs, which eat into profits and earnings. They can also pass those costs to consumers, who ultimately suffer from pricing inflation. So, while tariffs are neither good nor bad, there are complexities that come with rapidly reshoring. And there are still a lot of uncertainties with how that implementation will work.

Bottom Line

While the best action is typically to stick to long-term goals, short-term fluctuations do matter. They help us decide what tactical allocations to make - whether that is to hedge our current portfolio or add to existing holdings. And they also set a precedent to prepare us better for the next big market event.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2025. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.

data.title: Tesco: More To Lose As The Value Player In U.K. Price War
data.description:
Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDF) (OTCPK:TSCDY) had an alright year thanks to mix effects which we've been tracking in our coverage. The issue is that there is a price war coming in the British grocery sector, and while Tesco's Finest push has yielded benefits, price matching initiatives are limiting incremental growth. While not the cleanest comp because it crosses geographies, the outlook is better for companies like Kroger (KR) who at least seem to have a better relative position to the value competitors in the US in the face of tariffs, meaning less incremental competition. With valuations between the two being similar, we prefer Kroger.

Tesco Results Just Reported

Tesco's sales growth was pretty limited. Pricing had already started to slow below inflation rates in its increases as of our previous coverage, despite the fact that inflation has proceeded reflecting the tighter economic conditions. Indeed, even at Tesco the wage growth rate was around 5.2%. Volumes were decent on their delivery business which they are trying to scale.

However, there has been considerable operating profit growth as calculated by the company accounting for some write-offs related to more persistently high-interest rates. Some of this is being driven by non-UK geographies, where Central Europe, only a few percentage points of the company's sales, grew 30% in operating profit for the year as that business scales. There has also been an ongoing savings programme now targeting another 500 million GBP in 2025, meaning more than 10% of current run-rate operating profits.

Relevant is their Finest line, which is Tesco's supermarket label more premium products that are growing by 15% in the year, supporting a solid positive mix effect. Tesco has done a good job of keeping the value proposition going, but of course, it comes at the cost of margins, with the ongoing price-matching policy against Aldi fueling an ongoing price competition that has led to Tesco's latest profit warning. Aldi is a hard discounter, Tesco was more neutrally positioned in the market before, but the price matching principle on hundreds of products is pushing Tesco more into the value segment where competition is more cutthroat. It's not that favourable of a movement within the industry structure.

Bottom-Line

Indeed, despite the considerable cost savings plan slated for the year, the guidance is pointing at a down year in terms of profits. Forecast operating profits are supposed to be between 2.7 and 3 billion GBP, down from the 3.13 billion GBP this year. This means there isn't a lot of incremental benefit expected from mix effects, and pricing must be under considerable pressure in the UK business assuming volumes can stay resilient. From what we could see, pricing wasn't disclosed in the latest figures, likely meaning that it is looking considerably worse than before as the competition heats up - likely entering negatively despite some amount of UK inflation continuing. Pricing has already been dampening sales in 2024. The volume was up.

Our thoughts then turn to alternative picks like Kroger, which in the US offer a more balanced grocer offering, as well as other more comprehensive stores, rather than being as aggressive on pricing. In that regard, Kroger actually saw a solid market response to the now-suspended reciprocal tariffs enacted by Trump, as tariffs would have relatively sharper harm on value competitors. Tesco and Kroger valuations are in the same neighbourhood as well, with Kroger at 14.56x against Tesco's 12.57x on a forward basis. Kroger continues to expect profit growth while Tesco doesn't.

We think that given the economic uncertainties related to an escalating trade war between the US and China, it is possible to think even more defensively with a better proposition in terms of value than these picks, but if we'd have to pick one, we'd probably take Kroger on a financial and operational basis. Though we are concerned that the US has more to lose incrementally as an economy that has been resilient these years, but that may be more implicated in the possible economic fallout that could come from a trade war which would affect consumers. Kroger's relative position may be good within the US market as opposed to Tesco's within the UK, but it's not a screaming bargain either.
